name: Court de Gébelin's Tarot — The Birth of the 'Book of Thoth' (1781)
description:
# Court de Gébelin's Plates (1781)

## At a glance

The hinge of the whole story. In 1781 the Protestant scholar **Antoine Court de Gébelin**
published an essay declaring the Tarot de Marseille a survival of the ancient Egyptian
**Book of Thoth**. He offered **no evidence** — and was wrong — but he converted a card
game into a "sacred text" and launched two centuries of occult tarot. Bundled with his
essay was a second piece by the **Comte de Mellet**, which first proposed *reading the
cards* and linking them to the Hebrew alphabet.

## Origin & dating

Published in volume 8 of de Gébelin's encyclopedic *Le Monde Primitif* (1781). The story
runs that he saw the tarot game being played in a Paris salon (Mme Helvétius's) and
"recognised" Egyptian allegory in the trumps. His redrawn plates "corrected" the
Marseille images toward what he imagined Egyptian originals looked like.

## Provenance & evidence

The text and plates survive and are public-domain. What does **not** exist is any
evidentiary basis: this predates Champollion's decipherment of hieroglyphs (1822), so
de Gébelin's "Egyptian" reading was pure imagination projected onto Italian Christian
imagery.

## Game or divination?

This is the **birth of the divinatory/occult claim** — but as *theory*, not practice.
De Gébelin allegorised; it was the Comte de Mellet (and then Etteilla) who turned the
idea into a method. The grammar should treat de Gébelin as the **origin of the myth**,
explicitly dated 1781, and never as evidence about the decks' actual 15th-century origin.

## The fear question

**No fear at all — the opposite.** De Gébelin published openly, under his name, in a
respectable subscription encyclopedia read by the Parisian elite (Franklin was a
subscriber). Far from hiding "occult" knowledge, he *announced* it as prestigious
ancient wisdom. The 18th-century salon treated this as fashionable erudition, not
dangerous magic. The occult overlay was, at its birth, a **public, high-status
intellectual fad**.

## Counter-voices

This file is where the "suppressed occult history" claim must be weighed most carefully.
De Gébelin is the *source* of the Egyptian-origin belief that many practitioners still
hold. Defenders argue he intuited a real esoteric substrate. The scholarly verdict
(Decker, Depaulis, Dummett) is firm: the Egyptian genealogy is **invented**, not
recovered — a Romantic-Enlightenment fantasy about Egypt, not a transmission from it.
Importantly, debunking de Gébelin does **not** debunk the *real* later history of
divinatory practice or its persecuted practitioners; it only removes a false pedigree.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Clearly separate de Gébelin's essay from Mellet's appended essay — they differ (e.g.
  trump-to-Hebrew-letter assignments).
- The plates are interpretive redraws; flag them as such, not as historical decks.

## Sources & further reading

- "Antoine Court de Gébelin," Wikipedia; "De Gébelin's Tarot Essay in Le Monde Primitif,"
  Mary K. Greer.
- Decker, Depaulis & Dummett, *A Wicked Pack of Cards* (1996).

---
*Research notes — AI-assisted first-pass draft, pending review by the maintainer and the Tarot History Forum. Skeptical, document-first (Dummett spine). Corrections welcome via pending edit.*

sections.Iconography: A traveller in motley with a staff and bundle, a beast at his leg — the Marseille Fool, copied into the plate.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Court de Gébelin reads the unnumbered card as Folly (la Folie) — the senseless man, outside the sacred sequence.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A figure at a table of cups, coins and knives, wand raised.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: He reads the Bateleur as the active, material principle — the conjuror, the maker, the first agent of creation.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A veiled, enthroned woman in a tiara with a book.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: For Court de Gébelin she is the HIGH PRIESTESS of Egypt — sacred wisdom in female form, not a scandalous 'popess'.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A crowned woman with sceptre and eagle shield.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Queen, or the female sovereign principle of the Egyptian state and nature's fertility.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. sections.Iconography: A bearded sovereign in profile with sceptre and shield.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The King — temporal sovereignty among the Egyptians.

sections.Iconography: A tiara'd pontiff blessing two acolytes.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Court de Gébelin makes him the GRAND HIEROPHANT — the chief priest of the Egyptian mysteries.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: He reads it as Marriage, and as the man choosing between Vice and Virtue — Hercules at the crossroads.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A crowned prince in a canopied chariot drawn by two horses.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Osiris triumphant, or the King in his war-chariot — victory and dominion.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Sage or Hermit seeking truth — Court de Gébelin sometimes names him the Egyptian seeker of wisdom (the 'Capuchin').

items.name: 10 — Wheel of Fortune (La Roue de Fortune)

illustrations.scene: Wheel of Fortune (La Roue de Fortune)

sections.Iconography: A wheel turned by figures rising and falling.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The wheel of destiny — the turning of fortune the Egyptian priests taught.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A woman calmly parting a lion's jaws.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Fortitude — the second of the three cardinal Virtues among the sacred cards.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A youth suspended by one foot from a frame.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Court de Gébelin believed the card was printed UPSIDE-DOWN: rightly turned, he says, it shows Prudence, the fourth cardinal Virtue, with a raised foot.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A skeleton with a scythe among the fallen — the card left unnamed in the Marseille.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Death — the great Egyptian passage; he notes the Marseille leaves it nameless out of dread.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A winged figure pouring liquid between two vessels.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Temperance — the third of the cardinal Virtues set among the sacred cards.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A horned, winged figure over two bound captives.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: TYPHON — the evil principle of Egyptian theology, the destroyer set against Osiris.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: Figures fall from a tower struck by fire from the sky.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: He reads the 'Maison-Dieu' as the Castle of Plutus, or the temple of pride struck down — calamity and the fall of avarice.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A naked woman kneels pouring two urns into water and onto land, beneath a great star and seven small ones.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: His most famous reading: the great star is SIRIUS (Sothis), the Dog-Star whose rising herald the flooding of the NILE; the woman pours the inundation that renews Egypt.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A dripping moon over two dogs and a crayfish between two towers.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Moon and its dew; he reads the falling drops as the influence of the moon on the Egyptian year.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A radiant sun over two children by a wall.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Sun — Osiris as the day-star, the source of Egyptian life.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: An angel trumpets while the dead rise from a tomb.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Creation, or the Last Judgement — the awakening of the dead the Egyptians taught.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

sections.Iconography: A dancing figure in a wreath, the four beasts in the corners.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: TIME, or the Universe — Court de Gébelin reads the central figure as Isis and the four beasts as the four seasons/elements: the whole created world.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.

items.name: The 'Book of Thoth' — The Birth of Occult Tarot

sections.What happened here: In 1781 Antoine Court de Gébelin looked at an ordinary Tarot de Marseille — a card game — and declared it the last survivor of an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth', a hieroglyphic encyclopaedia of priestly wisdom carried west by the 'Gypsies'. He was wrong on every count: tarot is a 15th-century Italian game, the 'Egyptian' reading is invented, and the Romani have no link to Egypt. Hieroglyphs would not be deciphered for another 40 years.
sections.Why it matters: And yet this single essay created esoteric tarot. Within a decade Etteilla built the first divination deck on it; Lévi added the Kabbalah; Wirth, the Golden Dawn, Waite, and Crowley followed. Every occult tarot — and the whole idea that tarot tells fortunes — descends from this beautiful mistake. The companion essay by the Comte de Mellet, in the same volume, first proposed the Hebrew-letter associations and the reverse-order Egyptian cosmogony.
